LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT WILLIAM MORA'S MOTION FOR AN ORDER DECLARING BRYANT FU A VEXATIOUS LITIGANT, FOR SECURITY, AND FOR PREFILING ORDER. DEFENDANT WILLIAM MORA'S MOTION FOR AN ORDER DECLARING PLAINTIFF BRYANT FU A VEXATIOUS LITIGANT, FOR SECURITY, AND FOR PREFILING ORDER IS DENIED. DEFENDANT HAS NOT SHOWN THAT PLAINTIFF IS A VEXATIOUS LITIGANT WITHIN THE MEANING OF CIVIL CODE SECTION 391(B)(3). FURTHER, BECAUSE THE COURT HAS SUSTAINED THE DEMURRER TO THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND, IT DENIES DEFENDANT'S REQUEST THAT IT REQUIRE PLAINTIFF TO FURNISH SECURITY UNDER CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 391.1, A PROVISION WHICH ONLY APPLIES TO "PENDING" LITIGATION "AT ANY TIME UNTIL FINAL JUDGMENT IS ENTERED." THE PREVAILING PARTY SHALL SUBMIT A [PROPOSED] WRITTEN ORDER THAT TRACKS THE COURT'S RULING, VERBATIM. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: J. Mr. Fu stipulates to the judge pro tem. Both Mr. Fu and the judge pro tem authorize the clerk to sign the stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The following motions are before the Court: (1 and 2) Cross-Defendant's motion for protective order and Cross-Complainant's to compel raise the same issues but by different means; (3) Cross-Defendant's motion to have requests 16, 17 and 18 be deemed admitted; (4) Cross-Defendant's motion have further responses to form interrogatories 17.1 with respect to requests 4, 5, 6, 8. 9, 12 and 15 and request for production of documents numbers 8 and 9. The Court makes the following rulings.

Cross-Complainant's objections to the timeliness of the motions is overruled based on the Court's emergency order tolling time limits in light of the COVID-19 pandemic.

(1and 2) Motion for protective order granted, motion to compel denied. Cross-complainant has made no showing that other cameras would depict the area in question based on the evidence submitted by Cross-Defendant and given the history between the parties and related parties, there is a potential that information regarding security surrounding Cross-Defendant's property could be used for improper purposes. In particular, the Court notes that Cross-Complainant has stated in discovery response that he doesn't know much about what happened or the damages he sustained, yet he has brought this action.

(3) No responses were made to requests of admissions 16, 17 and 18 either by way of admission or denial, objection, or protective order. To the extent that such admissions are deemed to not be relevant that will be determined at the time of trial, howver, the failure to respond waives all objections. (CCP 2033.280(a).) Therefore, the motion to have requests 16, 17 and 18 deemed admitted is granted without prejudice to Cross-Complainant's right to assert objections at trial to their admissibility.

(4) The motion to compel further answers to form interrogatories is granted as follows. With respect, to: 17.1(4) - Cross-Complainant Cross-Complainant needs to state all facts that support his contention pursuant to (b), provide contact information as to (c), and state information regarding documents or that none exist. 17.1(5) - Cross-Complainant needs to admit or deny whether he is challenging this contention based on the evidence he has (i.e. video) and then respond accordingly to the subparts;17.1(6), (8), (12) and (14) - Cross-Complainant needs to respond to subparts since he denied request. Further responses are to be made by July 27, 2020.

Request for sanctions is denied.

The Court instructs Mr. Fu to seek the opposing side's approval as to the form of the proposed order and thereafter to email the proposed order to the judge pro tem at rkane1089@aol.com.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-24-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Plaintiff Bryant Fu appears briefly prior to calendar call to confirm he does not contest the tentative ruling.

No other appearance is made in this matter

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT WILLIAM MORA DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Defendant William Mora's demurrer to the first amended complaint is sustained, as to Mr. Mora only, without leave to amend. Plaintiff alleges that in June 2014, he obtained a sanctions award against a non-party, Dennis Yan, for taking a frivolous appeal to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. (Compl. 18.) He alleges further that Yan fraudulently transferred assets to third parties, including Legal Recovery, LLC, to avoid paying that award. (Id. 21-23, 26.) The only charging allegations against Mr. Mora in the first amended complaint are that he is the agent for service of process of Legal Recovery, and is not a California resident, and that he has "actively rendered Legal Recovery unable to be served with process to frustrate [Plaintiff] and other creditors' attempts to recover assets Yan has hidden behind Legal Recovery." (Id. 29, 30.) Taking these allegations as true, as required on demurrer, they do not state a cause of action against Mora for fraud or conspiracy to defraud.

Liberally construing the first amended complaint, Plaintiff (who is representing himself in propria persona) appears to be attempting to state a cause of action against Mora for aiding and abetting a fraudulent transfer in violation of the former Uniform Fraudulent Transfer Act, Civ. Code 3439 et seq., which applied until January 1, 2016, when it was superseded by the Uniform Voidable Transactions Act with respect to transfers made on or after the effective date. (Civ. Code 3439.14(a).) However, Mora is not alleged to be the transferee of property fraudulently transferred by Yan, but only the agent for service of process for the entity to which the property was transferred, and he is not alleged to have committed any act in furtherance of the alleged conspiracy to defraud Plaintiff or to conceal Yan's assets from him. Further, Plaintiff alleges that the fraudulent transfer to Legal Recovery occurred "shortly after Plaintiff obtained his sanctions award against Yan" (id. 26) in June 2014, and that he discovered Yan's fraudulent use of Legal Recovery in March 2017. (Id. 22-24, 26.) As a result, any claim for fraudulent transfer would be time-barred, because the original complaint was filed on December 27, 2018, more than four years after the alleged fraudulent transfer (and more than one year after Plaintiff allegedly discovered Yan's use of Legal Recovery). (Civ. Code 3439.09 [a cause of action with respect to a fraudulent transfer is "extinguished" unless action is brought "within four years after the transfer was made or the obligation was incurred or, if later, within one year after the transfer or obligation was or could reasonably have been discovered by the claimant"].) The demurrer to the third cause of action is sustained because there is no independent cause of action for injunctive relief. Because Plaintiff has not shown any reasonable possibility that he can amend the complaint to state a cause of action, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend.

The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR No. 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com

PLAINTIFF BRYANT FU'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT

Defendant Mora's demurrer is sustained with 30 days leave to amend. Plaintiff fails to allege facts showing how Mr. Yan harmed him as opposed to his father. The real party in interest must prosecute an action per CCP 367. In addition, the claims are time-barred. The statute of limitations for fraud is 3 years under CCP 338(d). Plaintiff is complaining about transfers that occurred years ago and that have been the subject of prior suits between Tony Fu (plaintiff's father) and Mr. Yan. (See Complaint, 34, 45 and Complaint in CGC 15-549575, 44.)
